metadata.title: Count of Smaller Numbers After Self

learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Merge Sort, Recursion, Divide and Conquer, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine students standing in a queue based on arrival time. For every student, you want to know how many students standing behind them have a lower score. Instead of checking every student behind them individually, Merge Sort can count these values efficiently while sorting.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine students standing in a queue based on arrival time. For every student, you want to know how many students standing behind them have a lower score. Instead of checking every student behind them individually, Merge Sort can count these values efficiently while sorting.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this Modified Merge Sort whenever you need to count relationships between elements appearing before and after each index.
learning.signals: Whenever you need to count relationships between elements appearing before and after each index, think of Modified Merge Sort or Binary Indexed Tree.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums, return an integer array counts where counts[i] is the number of smaller elements to the right of nums[i].

examples.explanation: 5 has two smaller elements (2,1). 2 has one smaller element (1). 6 has one smaller element (1). 1 has none.
There are no elements after it.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: For every element, compare it with every element to its right and count how many are smaller.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: While merging two sorted halves, every time an element from the right half moves before an element in the left half, it means that element is smaller and originally appeared after it. Count how many such right elements have already moved before placing each left element.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: Every right element that moves before a left element during merging is a smaller element appearing after it.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever you need to count relationships between elements appearing before and after each index, think of Modified Merge Sort or Binary Indexed Tree.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Each merge operation counts all smaller elements appearing after every index while sorting. Every level performs linear work, giving O(n log n) overall complexity.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever you need to count relationships between elements appearing before and after each index, think of Modified Merge Sort or Binary Indexed Tree.
commonWrongPaths: Updating counts at the wrong time.

hints: Merge Sort can count while sorting.
commonMisconceptions: Updating counts at the wrong time.

pattern.name: Modified Merge Sort
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever you need to count relationships between elements appearing before and after each index, think of Modified Merge Sort or Binary Indexed Tree.

approaches.title: Nested Loop Comparison
approaches.idea: For every element, compare it with every element to its right and count how many are smaller.
algorithm: Compare nums[i] with every j > i.

approaches.title: Binary Indexed Tree
approaches.idea: Compress the values and use a Fenwick Tree to count how many smaller values have already been processed while traversing from right to left.

approaches.title: Modified Merge Sort
approaches.idea: Store every element together with its original index. During Merge Sort, whenever elements from the right half move before elements from the left half, count how many right elements have already been placed. Add that count to the answer for every left element.
approaches.intuition: Each merge operation counts all smaller elements appearing after every index while sorting. Every level performs linear work, giving O(n log n) overall complexity.
algorithm: Whenever right element is smaller, increment rightCounter.
When placing a left element, add rightCounter to its answer.
approaches.dryRun:
nums=[5,2,6,1]
Split
Merge [5],[2] -> count[5]+=1
Merge [6],[1] -> count[6]+=1
Final Merge -> count[5]+=1
Answer=[2,1,1,0]

code.python: Uses modified Merge Sort with index tracking.
code.cpp: Uses Merge Sort with original indices.
code.javascript: Uses Merge Sort with pair objects.

visualization.description: Count how many right-side elements move ahead during Merge Sort.

interviewFollowUps: Can this be solved using a Fenwick Tree?
How does Segment Tree solve this problem?
Why must original indices be stored?
How does this differ from Count Inversions?
interviewTips: Mention why original indices are necessary.
Explain why Merge Sort achieves O(n log n).

cheatSheet.keyObservation: Every right element that moves before a left element during merging is a smaller element appearing after it.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Store Indices → Merge Sort → Count During Merge.

commonMistakes: Updating counts at the wrong time.
